# Last Time

::: {.incremental}
+ **Advanced cin**
+ **Dates and Random Numbers**
+ **IPO Models**
+ **Execution Trace Models**
+ **Terminal Charts and Graphs**
:::

::: notes
Brief recall of lecture 03.01. Students should remember that IPO models plan a program before code, execution traces inspect code after it exists, and formatted terminal output can make data easier to read.
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

# Today's Agenda

- Vectors
- Vector Operations
- Range-Based For Loops

::: notes

:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

{{< include topic_1_vectors.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_2_vector_operations.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_4_range_based_for.qmd >}}

---

# Key Takeaways

- A vector stores many related values of the same type under one name.
- `push_back`, `pop_back`, and `size` change or inspect the vector.
- Range-based `for` is the beginner-friendly way to read every value.
- IPO models and execution traces help explain why the vector exists and how it changes.

<!-- Slide 40 -->

---

# Next Time

- More practice with range-based loops
- Inputting vector data
- Processing vector contents

<!-- Slide 41 -->

---

# Appendix

<!-- Slide 42 -->

---

## Vectors: Good Practices

- Use plural names for vectors, such as `scores`, `names`, or `items`.
- Choose the vector element type before choosing the variable name.
- Use vectors when values belong together as one ordered group.

<!-- Slide 43 -->

---

## Vectors: Common Mistakes

- Forgetting `#include <vector>`.
- Mixing different value types in one vector.
- Treating the vector as a single value instead of a container.

<!-- Slide 44 -->

---

## Vectors: More Examples

```cpp
vector<int> scores = {90, 85, 100};
vector<double> prices = {1.25, 2.50, 4.99};
vector<string> names = {"Maya", "Ari", "Chen"};
```

<!-- Slide 45 -->

---

## Vectors: Practice Problems

1. Declare a vector named `ages` that stores integers.
2. Declare a vector named `prices` with three starting values.
3. Describe an IPO model for a program that stores three names.

<!-- Slide 46 -->

---

## Vectors: AI Search Terms

- "C++ vector beginner"
- "C++ include vector"
- "C++ vector initialization"

<!-- Slide 47 -->

---

## Vectors: Questions for Reflection

- Why is a vector more useful than three separate variables?
- Why must every value in one vector have the same type?

<!-- Slide 48 -->

---

## Vector Operations: Good Practices

- Use `push_back` when values are discovered one at a time.
- Check `size()` before reading from a vector.
- Remember that `pop_back()` removes the last value.

<!-- Slide 49 -->

---

## Vector Operations: Common Mistakes

- Expecting `pop_back()` to print or return the removed value.
- Calling `pop_back()` on an empty vector.
- Forgetting that `size()` changes after adding or removing values.

<!-- Slide 50 -->

---

## Vector Operations: More Examples

```cpp
vector<string> names;

names.push_back("Maya");
names.push_back("Ari");

cout << names.size() << endl;
```

<!-- Slide 51 -->

---

## Vector Operations: Practice Problems

1. Trace a vector after three `push_back` statements.
2. Trace a vector after one `pop_back` statement.
3. Write an IPO model for storing three prices.

<!-- Slide 52 -->

---

## Vector Operations: AI Search Terms

- "C++ vector push_back"
- "C++ vector pop_back"
- "C++ vector size"

<!-- Slide 53 -->

---

## Vector Operations: Questions for Reflection

- Why does `push_back` add to the end instead of the beginning?
- How does an execution trace help explain vector changes?

<!-- Slide 54 -->

---

## Range-Based For Loops: Good Practices

- Use range-based `for` when reading every value.
- Give the vector a plural name and the loop variable a singular name.
- Keep the repeated action inside the loop body.

<!-- Slide 55 -->

---

## Range-Based For Loops: Common Mistakes

- Forgetting the colon between the loop variable and the vector.
- Expecting the loop variable to exist after the loop ends.
- Expecting changes to the copied loop variable to change the vector.

<!-- Slide 56 -->

---

## Range-Based For Loops: More Examples

```cpp
vector<double> prices = {1.25, 2.50, 4.99};

for (double price : prices) {
    cout << "$" << price << endl;
}
```

<!-- Slide 57 -->

---

## Range-Based For Loops: Practice Problems

1. Read a range-based loop out loud in English.
2. Write a loop that prints every name in `names`.
3. Trace the loop variable for `{10, 20, 30}`.

<!-- Slide 58 -->

---

## Range-Based For Loops: AI Search Terms

- "C++ range based for loop"
- "C++ for each vector"
- "C++ loop variable scope"

<!-- Slide 59 -->

---

## Range-Based For Loops: Questions for Reflection

- Why is range-based `for` easier for every value?
- Why is the loop variable unavailable after the loop body?

<!-- Slide 60 -->
